title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to allow the child tax credit with respect to stillbirths.
plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules to help families who lose a baby before birth. If a family has a stillbirth after carrying the baby for at least 20 weeks, they can get the child tax credit. The child tax credit gives families up to $2,000 to help with costs. Right now, only living children can be counted for this tax credit, but this bill would include stillborn babies too.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1 Child Tax Credit Allowed With Respect to Stillbirths
sections.text: This section changes the tax law to let parents claim the child tax credit when they have a stillbirth. The law adds new rules to the part of the tax code that deals with the child tax credit. Under these new rules, if a parent has a stillbirth, they can treat that unborn child as if it were a qualifying child for the tax credit. This means they can get the same tax benefit they would have gotten if the baby had been born alive and then died right after birth. The law removes certain requirements that would normally prevent parents from claiming this credit. For example, parents would not need to have a taxpayer identification number or social security number for the unborn child if the child would have been eligible for these numbers if born alive. The law defines stillbirth as the delivery of a baby that died in the womb after the mother was pregnant for twenty weeks or more. An unborn child means any human baby at any stage of development while still in the mother's womb. These changes apply to tax years that end after this law is passed.
